title: Lighting Fundamentals
description: Lighting is what transforms a flat colored surface into something that reads as three-dimensional. This lesson builds the classic Blinn-Phong model from scratch: starting with the surface normal, computing the diffuse N·L term, and adding a specular highlight using the half-vector. The sphere geometry provides real interpolated normals to work with.

theory.key_concept: The fragment shader on a sphere receives `v_normal` — the interpolated surface normal in world space — and `v_position` — the world-space position of the fragment. These come from the vertex shader and are declared as `in vec3` varyings.
theory.normal_viz: Visualizing normals as RGB (mapping [-1,1] to [0,1]) is the universal first debugging step. A normal pointing +X is red, +Y is green, +Z is blue. If the sphere shows a smooth color gradient from dark red at left to bright red at right, the normals are correct.
theory.diffuse: Lambertian diffuse intensity is `max(0.0, dot(N, L))` where N is the normalized surface normal and L is the normalized direction toward the light. It is zero on surfaces facing away from the light and peaks at 1.0 on surfaces directly facing the light.
theory.blinn_phong: Blinn-Phong adds a specular highlight using the half-vector H = normalize(L + V), where V is the view direction (toward the camera). Specular intensity is `pow(max(0, dot(N, H)), shininess)`. Higher shininess values produce tighter, sharper highlights.

challenges.title: Normal Visualization
challenges.description: Visualize the sphere's surface normals as colors. Map the normal components from [-1, 1] to [0, 1] and output them as RGB. The sphere should display a smooth rainbow-like gradient that reveals its 3D curvature.
hints: Normals are in [-1, 1]. To display them as colors (which live in [0, 1]) use `N * 0.5 + 0.5`. This maps -1 to 0 and +1 to 1.
The left side of the sphere has normals pointing toward -X (negative red), so that region should appear dark red. The top points toward +Y — bright green. The center face points toward +Z — blue.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Normal visualization is the first thing professional shader developers do when setting up a new mesh or lighting rig. It confirms that normals are in the right space (world, view, or tangent) and pointing in the right direction. If your lighting looks wrong later, visualizing N is almost always the first debugging step.

hlsl.note: Varyings become struct members with semantics in HLSL.

challenges.title: Lambertian Diffuse
challenges.description: Implement Lambertian diffuse shading. The surface color should be proportional to `max(0.0, dot(N, L))` where L is the light direction from `u_light`. Give the sphere a warm orange base color and let the lighting dim it on surfaces facing away from the light.
hints: `dot(N, L)` returns the cosine of the angle between the normal and the light direction. When they point the same way (surface faces the light), the result is 1.0. When perpendicular, 0.0. When facing away, negative — clamp it to 0.
Use `max(0.0, dot(N, L))` to prevent negative light contribution from behind-facing surfaces.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Lambertian diffuse is physically motivated: it models perfectly rough (matte) surfaces that scatter incoming light equally in all directions. The N·L dot product is a direct application of Lambert's cosine law — the same law that explains why the sun heats the equator more than the poles.

challenges.title: Ambient + Diffuse
challenges.description: Add an ambient term so surfaces that face away from the light aren't completely black. Combine a small ambient constant (0.08) with the Lambertian diffuse, keeping the orange base color.
hints: Ambient is a flat minimum light level that represents indirect illumination from the environment. Just add it to the diffuse term: `ambient + diff`.
A value around 0.05–0.15 is typical. Too high and shadows disappear; too low and unlit areas go pure black, which rarely looks natural.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Ambient light is a crude but effective approximation of global illumination — the bounced, scattered light that reaches even surfaces not directly facing any light source. In production, ambient is often replaced by an ambient occlusion map or an irradiance environment map (a blurred cube map), but the concept of a base light floor remains the same.

challenges.title: Blinn-Phong Specular
challenges.description: Add a Blinn-Phong specular highlight to complete the lighting model. The camera is at (0, 0, 3). Compute the half-vector H = normalize(L + V) and use `pow(max(0, dot(N, H)), 64.0)` for a tight white specular highlight. Combine ambient (0.08) + diffuse + specular.
hints: The half-vector H bisects the angle between the light direction L and the view direction V: `H = normalize(L + V)`. It's cheaper than computing the true reflection vector.
`pow(max(0, dot(N, H)), shininess)` — higher shininess (64, 128, 256) gives a tighter, more mirror-like highlight. Lower values (8, 16) give broad, plastic-like gloss.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Blinn-Phong dominated real-time rendering for decades and is still used in mobile games and stylized renderers today. The key insight is that all three terms — ambient, diffuse, specular — are computed per-fragment using only dot products, making them trivially parallel on GPU. The next step up is physically-based rendering (PBR), which replaces these empirical terms with energy-conserving BRDFs.
